title: Project Export to JSON

story.i_want_to: export a complete project as a JSON document that includes all epics, stories with full fields, and dependency edges
story.so_that: I can snapshot the project state for backup, migration to another loopctl instance, or feeding into analysis tools

rationale: Export is the complement to import (US-12.1). Together they enable round-trip fidelity: export a project, modify the JSON, re-import. Export also serves as a backup mechanism and a way to share project structures between tenants or instances. The export format matches the import format exactly, so the output of export can be directly used as input to import.

acceptance_criteria.description: GET /api/v1/projects/:id/export returns a JSON document containing the complete project structure: project metadata, epics (with all fields), stories (with all fields including agent_status, verified_status, assigned_agent_id, timestamps), and story_dependencies (as story number pairs).
The export format matches the import format (US-12.1) for round-trip fidelity. An export can be directly POSTed to import on the same or different project/tenant.

Stories include all fields: number, title, description, acceptance_criteria, estimated_hours, agent_status, verified_status, assigned_agent_id, assigned_at, reported_done_at, verified_at, rejected_at, rejection_reason, metadata.
Dependencies are expressed as story number pairs (e.g., {story: '2.1', depends_on: '1.3'}) rather than UUIDs, for portability across instances.
The response includes an export_metadata object: {exported_at: ISO8601, loopctl_version: string, project_id: uuid, tenant_id: uuid}.
Epic dependencies are included in the export as epic number pairs, similar to story dependencies.
The endpoint is accessible to agent role and above (agent, orchestrator, user). Tenant-scoped via RLS.
Returns 404 if the project does not exist or does not belong to the authenticated tenant.
The export includes epics ordered by number, stories within each epic ordered by number, and dependencies ordered consistently for deterministic output.

test_cases.name: export returns complete project structure
preconditions: fixture(:epic, %{project_id: project.id, tenant_id: tenant.id, number: 1, title: 'Epic 1'})
fixture(:story, %{epic_id: epic.id, tenant_id: tenant.id, number: '1.1', title: 'Story 1.1', agent_status: :implementing})
fixture(:story, %{epic_id: epic.id, tenant_id: tenant.id, number: '1.2', title: 'Story 1.2', agent_status: :pending})
fixture(:story_dependency, %{story_id: story_1_2.id, depends_on_story_id: story_1_1.id, tenant_id: tenant.id})
expected_results: Epic contains 2 stories ordered by number

test_cases.name: round-trip fidelity — export then import
preconditions: fixture(:project, %{tenant_id: tenant.id}) with 2 epics, 5 stories, 3 dependencies
steps: POST /api/v1/projects/:target_id/import with the export response body
expected_results: Target project has the same number of epics, stories, and dependencies as source
Story numbers, titles, and descriptions match between source and target
Dependencies are correctly recreated in the target project

test_cases.name: export includes all story fields
preconditions: fixture(:story, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, epic_id: epic.id, agent_status: :reported_done, verified_status: :rejected, rejection_reason: 'Missing tests', metadata: %{priority: 'high'}})
expected_results: Story includes timestamp fields (assigned_at, reported_done_at, rejected_at)

test_cases.name: tenant isolation on export
steps: GET /api/v1/projects/<tenant_b_project_id>/export with Tenant A's API key

test_cases.name: export with no epics or stories returns valid empty structure
preconditions: fixture(:project, %{tenant_id: tenant.id}) — no epics or stories

test_cases.name: export ordering is deterministic
preconditions: fixture(:project, %{tenant_id: tenant.id}) with 3 epics and multiple stories
expected_results: Both responses produce identical JSON (same ordering of epics, stories, dependencies)
Epics are ordered by number ascending
Stories within each epic are ordered by number ascending

technical_notes: Create Loopctl.Export module with export_project/1 that loads the project with all associations and serializes to the import-compatible JSON format.
Use Ecto preloads: Repo.preload(project, [epics: [stories: []], epic_dependencies: [], story_dependencies: []]). Order by number.
Dependencies must be expressed as story numbers (not IDs). Build a story_id -> story_number map from the loaded data and convert dependency edges.

Export JSON schema: {export_metadata: {...}, project: {name, slug, ...}, epics: [{number, title, ..., stories: [{number, title, ...}]}], story_dependencies: [{story, depends_on}], epic_dependencies: [{epic, depends_on}]}.
Controller: add export action to ProjectController or create ExportController. Route: GET /api/v1/projects/:id/export.
The loopctl_version in export_metadata should come from Mix.Project.config()[:version] or an Application config value.
For large projects (1000+ stories), consider streaming the JSON response or setting appropriate timeouts. For v1, a simple Jason.encode! should suffice.
Ensure the export format is documented (in the export_metadata or a separate schema doc) so consumers know the structure.

Testing infrastructure: (1) All tests use async: true via DataCase/ConnCase, (2) Mox mocks defined in test/support/mocks.ex, (3) Default permissive stubs set in DataCase/ConnCase setup via stub_all_defaults/0 and Mox.set_mox_from_context(tags) for async isolation, (4) setup :verify_on_exit! in every test module, (5) Fixtures in test/support/fixtures.ex with build/2 (no DB) and fixture/2 (DB insert with auto-dependency resolution), (6) NEVER Application.put_env in tests — all service swapping via config/test.exs.
All context functions MUST return `{:ok, result}` or `{:error, reason}` tuples. Never raise exceptions for expected error cases.
